themes.name: Rabbit Holes Worth Falling Down
themes.tagline: History, myths, and hidden systems that make the world click into place.

items.title: Fast Food Chains You Thought Were Gone
items.description:
If you watch Weird History Food, you know that plenty of fast food places have crashed, burned, and vanished. But some of the most famous chains you probably assumed were gone... never actually disappeared.

On this episode of Weird History Food, we take a look at the iconic burger joints and nostalgic restaurant brands that are still in business... even if they're hiding in plain sight.

What's your favorite presumably defunct fast food chain? Let us know in the comments!

(Much like real histo
items.why_watch: Rather than a standard eulogy for dead chains, this video flips the premise: these brands didn't actually die, they just became nearly invisible. It's a satisfying mix of nostalgia and genuine surprise for anyone who assumed certain childhood staples were long gone.

items.title: Historian Explores Famous Viking Sites in Iceland
items.description:
Historian Dan Snow explores the most iconic viking sites in Iceland!

You can now become a History Hit member right here on YouTube! Join for access to a new exclusive documentary every week, and access to over 160+ of our documentaries presented by world-renowned historians like Dan Snow, Eleanor Janega, Tristan Hughes, Mary Beard, Matt Lewis and more.

Get an exclusive release every week by signing up here: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCZwU2G-KVl-P-O-B35chZOQ/join

#iceland #vikings #visit
items.why_watch: Dan Snow visits the actual physical sites — longhouses, assembly plains, volcanic landscapes — rather than just narrating history from a studio, which gives the Viking settlement of Iceland a tangible sense of place. At 26 minutes it's long enough to feel like a proper trip without overstaying its welcome.

items.why_watch: Karolina Żebrowska zeroes in on a specific pattern: the historical myths that tend to survive are disproportionately the ones that frame women as passive victims of their own clothing. She backs the argument with specific examples and her dry, precise delivery keeps it from feeling like a lecture.

items.title: Why the US has so many undocumented immigrants
items.why_watch: In under three minutes, Johnny Harris distills the structural reasons the US undocumented population stays large — not individual choices but system design — making it one of the more efficient explainers on a topic that usually generates more heat than clarity.

themes.name: Beautiful Chaos, Beautiful Life
themes.tagline: Storms, symphonies, and stories of people building something real out of the mess.

items.title: The harmony Carnie Wilson spent her whole life trying to find
items.description:
We created this video in partnership with Unlikely Collaborators. 

Growing up as Brian Wilson's daughter meant music everywhere, and very little peace. In the first episode of The Me I Used to Be, Carnie Wilson opens up about what she was really looking for all those years, and how she finally found it.

Become a Big Think member to unlock expert classes, premium print issues, exclusive events and more: https://bigthink.com/membership/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=yt_desc
items.why_watch: Carnie Wilson recounts what it was like growing up inside Brian Wilson's world — music as both gift and chaos — and what she was actually chasing through all the public struggles she's been known for. It's a rare first-person account that reframes a familiar tabloid story as something quieter and more honest.

items.why_watch: Storm Goretti hits the chateau mid-renovation, and this episode documents the real damage and the team's immediate response — not a dramatic retelling after the fact. For followers of the project it's a grounding reminder that restoring a 19th-century French chateau is genuinely unpredictable work.

items.title: A BREAKDOWN of ALL Symbols, Themes, & the Ending of 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple
items.description: In this 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple Explained / The Bone Temple Ending Explained and Analysis, I take a deep dive into the new 28 Years Later The Bone Temple movie, starring Jack O’Connell, Ralph Fiennes, Erin Kellyman, Alfie Williams, Chi Lewis-Parry, and Cillian Murphy. Directed by Nia DaCosta, the film is a perfect blend of drama, action, and zombie horror, exploring themes of religion, morality, and humanity. I believe this film is excellent in capturing the major theme of human compassi
items.why_watch: This is a structured breakdown of the symbolism and ending of *28 Years Later: The Bone Temple* for viewers who want to process what they just watched rather than just rewatch it. Lucas Blue focuses specifically on the religious and moral themes Nia DaCosta builds into the film, which aren't always obvious on a first viewing.

themes.name: Make It Yourself, Thank Yourself Later
themes.tagline: From soil to neural networks — the deeply satisfying art of understanding how things are actually built.

items.title: But what is cross-entropy? | Compression is Intelligence Part 2
items.description:
Where the loss function for training LLMs comes from.
Job opportunities aligned to this audience: https://3b1b.co/talent
Early views and other perks for supporters: https://3b1b.co/support
Home page: https://www.3blue1brown.com

Manim animations by Aaron Gostein and Grant Sanderson
NanoGPT animation by Clayton Rabideau
3d black-box model by Paul Dancstep
Music by Vince Rubinetti

Timestamps

0:00 - Language trees and zipping
3:02 - Recap optimal codes
5:20 - Defining cross-entropy
8:26 - Intuiti
items.why_watch: Grant Sanderson builds a genuine understanding of cross-entropy — the loss function at the heart of LLM training — by grounding it in information theory and compression rather than just handing you a formula. If you've wanted to understand *why* models are trained the way they are, not just how, this 34 minutes will actually get you there.

items.description:

And for your completely free abridged version of Ben's book, click here:
https://www.growveg.com/grow-book-download

For out tour of Charles Dowding's garden where Ben literally takes a deep dive into compost, see: https://youtu.be/Nl4re8crECs 

Everything is getting more expensive. And that includes potting mix or compost, which is one of the humble vegetable gardener's greatest
items.why_watch: The video makes a concrete, cost-driven case for making your own growing medium rather than buying bagged potting mix, walking through exactly what to combine and why. Practical enough to change what you do this season, especially as compost-based alternatives keep proving themselves in real garden trials.

morning_soundtrack.title: Tiny Desk Radio: Air, Igor Levit, Fito Páez
morning_soundtrack.snippet: With the buzz of the final in the air, we celebrate the beautiful game with music from countries that have won the World Cup in recent years. Look for Tiny Desk Radio on your local NPR station.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The feeling of watching strangers celebrate in a sun-drenched plaza, flags waving, strangers briefly becoming family.

morning_soundtrack.snippet: The creator and long-running host of the influential show helped spearhead alt-rock’s mainstream explosion

Brook joins us in the midst of preparing to present the Cobalt Blue material live for the first time in years to discuss t
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The sonic equivalent of light refracting through deep water, cool and weightless and ancient.

The pop rock singer-songwriter is embarking on his first arena tour. Learn how to get tickets, deals, and everything else to know.
Sombr’s “You Are the Reason Tour”: How to Get Last-Minute Tickets
Travis Bland
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The breathless scramble of last-minute plans that somehow always turn into the night you'll talk about for years.

The late Clem Burke contributed heavily to their first studio album since 2014's Meteorites.
Echo & the Bunnymen Announce New Album Apples for Isaac, Reveal Lead Single: Stream
Eddie Fu

global_silver_linings.title: Japanese Engineers Help Modernize India’s Massive Railway, Launching Her First Bullet Train
global_silver_linings.snippet: For the most train-dependent transportation system in the world, the debut of its first high-speed or “bullet” train, is now tantalizingly close. It’s set to begin phased passenger operations starting from August 15th, 2027, provided there are no more delays to the project. Set to connect the financial center of Mumbai with Ahmedabad in the […] The post Japanese Engineers Help Modernize India’s Massive Railway, Launching Her First Bullet Train appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: Two nations sharing engineering expertise across borders is a quiet but powerful reminder that collaboration can transform everyday life for millions of people. When a bullet train replaces a gruelling journey, it gives ordinary commuters something precious: time back with their families.

global_silver_linings.title: How a farming programme in Sierra Leone helps amputees rebuild lives
global_silver_linings.snippet: An empowering initiative teaches people with limb loss how to farm sustainably, enabling many to start businesses The post How a farming programme in Sierra Leone helps amputees rebuild lives appeared first on Positive News .
global_silver_linings.reason: This programme shows that rebuilding a life after devastating injury is possible when people are given practical tools, land, and community. Growing food is not just about sustenance — it's about restoring dignity and a sense of future.

discovery_articles.title: Hubbell Rubbell in Howard, Kansas
discovery_articles.snippet: The small grass lot along Highway 99 is packed with Jerry Hubbell's welded folk-art creatures, a whole menagerie built from farm equipment, scrap metal, and whatever else he had lying around. The cowboy, the dinosaur, the tin man, the giant insect and the pig in glasses all sharing space in this rusty universe. Jerry Hubbell was a rancher who learned welding only to repair farm implements, then he turned that skill into decades of outsider art until health issues finally forced him to stop.

discovery_articles.title: Man Sentenced for Stealing 'Rare and Unique' Chinese Texts, Valued at More Than $200,000, by Using Aliases, Fake IDs and Dummy Manuscripts
discovery_articles.snippet: The works were stolen from the University of California, Los Angeles, over the course of several years

discovery_articles.title: Giraffes Might Be Even Smarter Than We Thought. Some Can Solve Simple Math Problems, a New Study Suggests
discovery_articles.snippet: Two of four tested giraffes seemed to be able to mentally combine numbers to get one of their favorite treats: carrots. But all the animals failed at tasks involving subtraction or multiple calculations

discovery_articles.title: Astronomers Noticed a Mysterious Spot Appearing in Telescope Images. It Turned Out to Be the Faintest Exoplanet Ever Imaged From Earth
discovery_articles.snippet: The gas giant, called Beta Pictoris d, had been hiding in plain sight for more than a decade, until two independent teams verified its existence

discovery_articles.snippet: The proposal comes as A.I.-generated music makes up an increasing share of what’s available on streaming platforms

discovery_articles.title: An ancient Maya astronomer finally has a name
discovery_articles.snippet: A Maya calendar formula bears the name Sak Tahn Waax, the first known Classic Maya mathematician-astronomer directly credited for such work.

discovery_articles.title: Genes offer new clues to stopping Huntington’s disease in its tracks
discovery_articles.snippet: A new generation of therapies aims to intervene at a recently discovered gap between the disease’s molecular march and its neurological consequences.

discovery_articles.snippet: While Vienna’s coffeehouses bred modernism, in Belgrade’s kafanas grew conspiracy and rage. Their clash consumed Europe - by Anton Cebalo Read on Aeon

discovery_articles.title: The Debate Over Pictures in Science
discovery_articles.snippet: Botanical illustrations helped bring science to new audiences—but not every scientist welcomed the change. The post The Debate Over Pictures in Science appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

featured_read.title: How to Bear Your Sorrows: Nick Cave on Integrating the Darkness of Loss with the Bright Ongoingness of Life
featured_read.snippet: Few things in our culture are more wounding than the concept of healing — as if the pains and losses that we suffer are an illness, a malfunction of the psyche to be cut out like a tumor, rather than a natural function of being alive, of feeling life deeply and living it fully. All our sorrows have been wasted if we have not learned to suffer and to be surprised by the door at the end of our suffering, on the other wide of which is simply the world, which keeps on worlding. It is not healing that allows… read article
featured_read.blurb: Nick Cave argues that grief isn't a wound to be healed but a capacity to be carried — and that the real surprise waiting on the other side of sorrow isn't recovery, but the world, still stubbornly, beautifully worlding. Set aside the half-hour and let it quietly dismantle the idea that you were ever supposed to get over anything.

garden_note.note: Zürich's July evenings carry that particular golden warmth that lingers well past eight — the linden trees are still breathing their sweetness into the air, and the garden is quietly exhaling after another long sun-drenched day.
in_season: Lavender at peak bloom — cut stems before the lower florets drop
Courgettes swelling overnight — check beneath leaves at dusk
French beans ready to pick before they go leathery
Deadhead dahlias to keep the flush coming through August
garden_note.sky_tonight: A slim waxing crescent sets early in the west — look for it low above the Uetliberg ridge just after sunset, with Venus keeping it company nearby.

previous_puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Riddle
previous_puzzle.prompt:
I rise without waking, fall without sleep,
I paint the window gold before the house stirs.
I am loudest in silence, gone by your first cup.
What am I?

fern_data.greeting: As the last light folds itself behind the horizon and your open tabs begin to feel like small obligations, let tonight's reading be the gentle kind — Vikings and giraffes, Nick Cave and bullet trains, all waiting here like soft music through a half-open door.
